caveats: a: The occupational data are four-digit and include turbine and engine manufacturers with a different engineering mix.
a: NIST documents technical opportunities in machine tools, not the share of 333612 wages that can be substituted.
rho: No representative 333612 adoption or realized-savings dataset was found.
rho: Implementation here means operational capture of exposed labor opportunity, not general equipment productivity.

e: Industry establishment counts can include plants inside multi-industry machinery groups.

s5: Replacement-parts specialists and large OEM-program suppliers likely have different buyer pools and succession dynamics.

q: Retention should be higher in proprietary or urgent replacement work and lower in drawing-to-print production runs.

o: Automation may reduce staffing intensity substantially without eliminating the accountable operator, which this primitive distinguishes.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Speed Changer, Industrial High-Speed Drive, and Gear Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business sales of catalog, configured, engineered, replacement, and spare gearing and drives, priced by unit, drawing, program, or supply agreement, sometimes with engineering and aftermarket support bundled into the product relationship.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, non-control investment vehicles, motor-vehicle and aircraft-specific gearing classified elsewhere, hydrostatic drives, pure distributors, and repair-only businesses without manufacturing are excluded.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of gears, speed changers, and non-hydrostatic industrial high-speed drives that repeatedly supply external OEM, distributor, maintenance, repair, and industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for four-digit 333600 rather than wage-weighted task exposure at 333612. I map engineering analysis, quoting, design documentation, programming, planning, procurement, quality analysis, customer support, and portions of CNC monitoring and inspection to AI exposure, while discounting machining, assembly, heat treatment, maintenance, metrology execution, and material handling; turbines and engines in the source likely increase engineering intensity relative to many gear shops, biasing the proxy upward.
caveats: The occupational data are four-digit and include turbine and engine manufacturers with a different engineering mix.
NIST documents technical opportunities in machine tools, not the share of 333612 wages that can be substituted.
a.change_evidence: A current wage-weighted task census at independent 333612 firms, mapping payroll to O*NET and recording time in design, quoting, CNC programming, setup, inspection, quality documentation, scheduling, sales, administration, and physical production, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: In 333600, production occupations were 47.86% of employment in 2023, while engineering was 17.03%, management 7.84%, business and financial operations 6.23%, and computer and mathematical occupations 2.38%. Gear manufacturing combines physical machining and assembly with unusually information-rich design, process planning, CNC, inspection, and documentation workflows that AI can partly substitute or use to avoid hiring.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit 333600 engine, turbine, and power-transmission equipment, not constant-quality demand for 333612 or the LMM customer basket. I convert the reported 1.3% annual output growth into an approximately 1.07 five-year anchor and widen for segment mix, imports, inventories, and industrial cycles; turbine and engine trends could bias the source either direction for gears and drives.
caveats: The projection is four-digit and combines materially different turbines, engines, and power-transmission products.
Industry production can diverge from customer demand because of trade, inventory, and market-share changes.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar 333612 shipments, imports, exports, backlog, and replacement-versus-OEM mix, paired with end-market capex forecasts and customer fleet retirement plans, would materially change the five-year ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects 333600 real output from 38.0 to 43.1 over its ten-year projection period, a 1.3% annual increase, even as employment falls. Diverse installed machinery, replacement parts, automation, material handling, energy, and industrial investment support demand, while manufacturing cycles, imports, customer insourcing, and drivetrain redesign create downside.

caveats: The injected n of 56 is an ESTIMATE based on receipts and a sector margin rather than observed 333612 EBITDA.
Industry establishment counts can include plants inside multi-industry machinery groups.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level map from D&B Hoovers, Thomasnet, AGMA directories, company filings, ownership databases, customer mix, recurring aftermarket revenue, and audited normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The official industry scope closely matches repeat external industrial supply, and many specialized gear and drive makers can be standalone firms. Eligibility is reduced for captive divisions, firms controlled by larger groups, businesses with nonrecurring project economics, and receipts-bridged candidates whose actual normalized EBITDA is outside the target band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333612 ($846,117,672) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333612 ($4,176,707,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $389,909 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333612); class <5: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $439,045 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $86,141 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,628,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $319,571 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,307,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $648,833 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,981,571 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,958,384 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $55,097,905 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,810,209 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $145,794,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $28,604,893 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 56

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured operator-required share.
Automation may reduce staffing intensity substantially without eliminating the accountable operator, which this primitive distinguishes.
o.change_evidence: Commercial lights-out gear-cell benchmarks, staffing before and after advanced CNC, robotics, metrology, and AI deployment, and customer acceptance of autonomously released critical gears would materially move this value.
o.rationale: Remaining demand still requires an accountable manufacturer to select materials, machine teeth and bores, heat-treat, grind, inspect, certify, assemble, warrant, and deliver physical power-transmission products. Software can automate decisions and monitoring but cannot supply the current physical basket without an operating manufacturer.

caveats: No representative contract-level pass-through evidence was available.
Retention should be higher in proprietary or urgent replacement work and lower in drawing-to-print production runs.
q.change_evidence: Executed contracts, quote histories, qualification costs, renewal cadence, warranty terms, customer concentration, contribution margins, aftermarket pricing, and price response following documented productivity changes would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Customer qualification, embedded drawings, reliability history, low failure tolerance, switching costs, rapid replacement, and engineering support can protect benefits. OEM procurement, annual negotiations, should-cost analysis, competitive bids, and dual sourcing pass part of recurring savings to customers, especially for standardized gears.

rho.bridge: NIST's AIMS program targets real-time health and process prediction across U.S. machine tools, not five-year adoption by LMM gear makers. I treat it as proof of feasible monitoring and digital-twin workflows, then discount for legacy machines, weak sensor data, plant-specific models, integration, cybersecurity, precision validation, and workforce change; an active research program may overstate near-term production readiness.
caveats: No representative 333612 adoption or realized-savings dataset was found.
Implementation here means operational capture of exposed labor opportunity, not general equipment productivity.
rho.change_evidence: A plant survey of ERP, CAD/CAM, QMS, machine connectivity, sensor coverage, AI pilots, validation time, exception rates, capex, and verified labor hours avoided by workflow and firm size would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI can be deployed in quoting, drawing and specification review, engineering search, CNC assistance, inspection triage, scheduling, maintenance, and quality documentation. Precision, traceability, customer approval, sparse failure data, and the cost of integrating mixed-age equipment prevent full implementation, especially where volumes are low and parts are highly engineered.

caveats: Neither the qualifying transfer numerator nor eligible denominator is directly observed.
Replacement-parts specialists and large OEM-program suppliers likely have different buyer pools and succession dynamics.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year ownership-change census using PitchBook, CapIQ, Axial, GF Data, AGMA reports, corporate records, and plant permits, matched to all eligible firms and excluding closures, minority financings, and internal reorganizations, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: No qualifying-transfer cohort specific to LMM 333612 firms was found. Succession and strategic consolidation make transfers plausible, but customer qualification, owner engineering relationships, cyclicality, equipment condition, skilled labor, and a limited buyer set constrain the five-year rate.

principal_driver: Precision engineering and production create rich AI-assisted workflows while qualification and aftermarket urgency support repeat customer value.
principal_weakness: Four-digit evidence may overstate engineering exposure, and firm-level eligibility, transfer rates, and retained pricing benefits are not observed.
